title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
NPS or PPF: Which Investment Option Fits Your Financial Goals?
When it comes to building a secure financial future, most people look towards government schemes for stability. Since NPS and PPF are among the trusted government policies that can be used for retirement planning, people often get confused about which to pick. Understanding the difference between NPS and PPF will help you select the plan that aligns well with your financial goals. “In this article, we’ll break down both schemes, compare their similarities and differences to help you decide whether NPS or PPF fits your long-term plans better.
Understanding National Pension System (NPS)
The National Pension System is a government-backed retirement plan that helps you build a steady corpus over the years. You invest regularly, and the money grows through market-linked options. After retirement, you can take a lump sum and receive the rest as a pension. Every subscriber gets a unique PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number) to track contributions, and you can choose between two account types: Tier I (the primary retirement account with withdrawal limits) and Tier II (a flexible, optional account).
Key Features and Benefits of NPS
Here’s what stands out about NPS:
Government-sponsored savings plan open to salaried, self-employed, and unorganised sector workers
Market-linked growth instead of fixed returns
Lock-in period till you reach 60 years, with partial withdrawals allowed for specific needs like education, marriage, or medical emergencies
PRAN issued for seamless tracking of all investments
Two account options:
Tier I: Mandatory retirement account, minimum ₹500 deposit, limited withdrawals, tax benefits up to ₹2 lakh
Tier II: Optional account with a minimum ₹250 deposit, free withdrawals, and tax benefit only for government employees
Who Can Invest in NPS?
The NPS scheme is suitable for individuals looking to build disciplined long-term retirement savings. You’re eligible if you:
Are an Indian citizen (resident or non-resident)
Are between 18 and 70 years old
Can complete Know Your Customer (KYC) requirements
Are both mentally and financially fit
Want a structured, long-term, market-linked retirement plan
Understanding Public Provident Fund (PPF)
The Public Provident Fund is a long-standing government savings scheme designed for individuals who want stable, guaranteed returns. It’s beneficial for those outside EPF coverage, offering a safe way to build long-term wealth through a 15-year lock-in period. The scheme runs through post offices and selected banks, making it widely accessible. While PPF is known for steady interest, it also offers tax benefits.
Key Features and Benefits of PPF
Here’s what makes PPF stand out:
Long-term savings scheme with a 15-year lock-in and guaranteed returns
Current interest rate is 7.1% p.a., compounded annually and credited on 31st March
To earn maximum interest, deposits should ideally be made between the 1st and 5th of the month. You can use the PPF calculator to find out the interest earned and maturity amount depending on your investments.
Eligible for tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh a year under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act
Premature closure is allowed after 5 years for specific situations, such as education, medical emergencies, or a change in residency, along with a 1% interest reduction on the entire tenure.
Option to take a loan against the PPF balance; a new loan is possible after full repayment
Who Can Invest in PPF?
PPF is open to:
Any Indian citizen aged 18 or above
Guardians opening an account on behalf of a minor or someone of unsound mind
However, it’s not eligible for
Non-Resident Indians (NRIs)
Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs)
Anyone looking to open a joint account, as PPF allows only one account per individual.
Difference Between NPS and PPF
Here’s a quick NPS and PPF comparison to understand how they serve different types of long-term savers.
Similarities Between NPS and PPF
Although NPS or PPF work differently, they share a few core traits since both were designed to support long-term retirement planning:
Both function as long-term retirement-focused saving schemes.
You need to open an account before you can start contributing.
They come with a mandatory lock-in period.
Each offers tax advantages to encourage disciplined investing.
Returns aren’t taxed while they accumulate.
The final corpus is also tax-free at maturity.
Choosing Between NPS or PPF Based on Your Goals
To select any one of the above investment plans, it’s wise to check how it aligns with your financial objectives:
Retirement Planning Perspective
If your main goal is to build a bigger retirement nest egg, NPS generally has an edge over PPF because of its market-linked growth. For example, someone investing ₹1 lakh a year for 35 years could see a much larger corpus in NPS (assuming around 10% returns) than in PPF, which typically offers lower, fixed returns. Over such a long period, this difference compounds significantly, giving NPS the potential to create a stronger retirement fund.
Risk Appetite and Investment Horizon
NPS comes with some market risk because your money is partly invested in equities. However, it is tightly supervised by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA), so the chances of misuse are almost negligible. PPF, on the other hand, is backed entirely by the government, making its returns nearly risk-free.
When it comes to goals, NPS is a better option for long-term retirement planning. However, if you’re saving for other milestones, such as a child’s education or marriage, PPF is usually the more suitable option because it offers stable returns and predictable growth.
Key Takeaway
While both are long-term government-backed options, the difference between NPS and PPF lies in terms of returns, flexibility, and purpose.
Regulated by PFRDA, NPS offers Tier I (mandatory) and Tier II (optional) accounts to both Indians and NRIs, and gives market-linked growth with higher returns.
PPF provides guaranteed returns, a fixed 15-year lock-in, tax-free maturity, and is ideal for risk-averse individual Indian investors.
NPS allows partial withdrawals after 10 years, while PPF permits partial access after 5 years with limits.
Both schemes offer tax benefits, but NPS provides an additional deduction of ₹50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B).
Choosing between NPS or PPF depends on your goal; go with PPF for safety or NPS for higher retirement-focused growth.
Conclusion
Choosing between NPS or PPF really comes down to what you want from your money. If long-term growth and a retirement-focused plan appeal to you, NPS offers more potential for growth. But if you prefer stability and guaranteed returns, PPF keeps things simple and predictable. You may split your money between both options if you want a mix of stability and growth. So, choose a suitable option and start planning for retirement or other financial goals. There are online goal-oriented and retirement calculators that can help you find out the right savings to create a corpus that you need. Use these calculators and plan your finances effectively.
FAQs
Can I invest in both NPS and PPF at the same time?
You’re allowed to put money into both NPS and PPF. However, you should know that the combined tax deduction for these investments is capped at ₹1.5 lakh each year. NPS, however, provides an additional ₹50,000 deduction annually under Section 80CCD(1B).
Are NPS returns fully tax-free at maturity?
Not entirely. You may withdraw 60% of the NPS corpus tax-free, while 40% should be used to purchase an annuity, and that pension income is taxable. Only if your total corpus is ₹2 lakh or less can the full amount be withdrawn tax-free.
What risks are associated with NPS investments?
NPS carries market risk because your money is invested in assets like equities, corporate bonds, and government securities. So, returns can fluctuate depending on how these markets perform.
How does PPF differ from NPS in terms of safety?
PPF offers a fixed return set by the government, while NPS, regulated by PFRDA, provides market-linked returns (9-11% in the past). Since the returns are predictable, PPFs are considered safer than NPS.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
full_pdf_text:
NPS or PPF: Which Investment Option Fits Your Financial Goals?
When it comes to building a secure financial future, most people look for stability. Since NPS and PPF are among the government policies that can be used for retirement planning, people often get confused about which to pick. Understanding the difference between NPS and PPF will help you select the plan that aligns well with your financial goals. “In this article, we’ll break down both schemes, compare their similarities and differences to help you decide whether NPS or PPF fits your long-term plans better.
Understanding National Pension System (NPS)
The National Pension System is a government-backed retirement plan that helps you build a steady corpus over the years. You invest regularly, and the money grows through market-linked options. After retirement, you can take a lump sum and receive the rest as a pension. Every subscriber gets a unique PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number) to track contributions, and you can choose between two account types: Tier I (the primary retirement account with withdrawal limits) and Tier II (a flexible, optional account).
1. Key Features and Benefits of NPS
Here’s what stands out about NPS:
o Government-sponsored savings plan open to salaried, self-employed, and
unorganised sector workers o Market-linked growth instead of fixed returns o Lock-in period till you reach 60 years, with partial withdrawals allowed for specific
needs like education, marriage, or medical emergencies o PRAN issued for seamless tracking of all investments o Two account options:
▪ Tier I: Mandatory retirement account, minimum annual contribution
₹1,000, per‑transaction minimum ₹500, tax benefits up to ₹2 lakh
▪ Tier II: ₹1,000 initial contribution; Optional account with a minimum ₹250
per subsequent deposit, free withdrawals, and tax benefit only for government employees 2. Who Can Invest in NPS?
The NPS scheme is suitable for individuals looking to build disciplined long-term retirement savings. You’re eligible if you:
o Are an Indian citizen (resident or non-resident) o Are between 18 and 70 years old o Can complete Know Your Customer (KYC) requirements o Are legally competent to execute a contract under the Indian Contract Act o Want a structured, long-term, market-linked retirement plan
Understanding Public Provident Fund (PPF)
The Public Provident Fund is a long-standing government savings scheme designed for individuals who want stable, guaranteed returns. It’s beneficial for those outside EPF coverage, offering a safe way to build long-term wealth through a 15-year lock-in period. The scheme runs through post offices and selected banks, making it widely accessible. While PPF is known for steady interest, it also offers tax benefits.
1. Key Features and Benefits of PPF
Here’s what makes PPF stand out:
o Long-term savings scheme with a 15-year lock-in and guaranteed returns o Current interest rate is 7.1% p.a., compounded annually and credited on 31st March o To earn maximum interest, deposits should ideally be made between the 1st and 5th
of the month. You can use the PPF calculator to find out the interest earned and maturity amount depending on your investments. o Eligible for tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh a year under Section 80C of the Income
Tax Act o Premature closure is allowed after 5 years for specific situations, such as education,
medical emergencies, or a change in residency, along with a 1% interest reduction on the entire tenure. o Option to take a loan against the PPF balance; a new loan is possible after full
repayment 2. Who Can Invest in PPF?
PPF is open to:
o Any Indian citizen aged 18 or above o Guardians opening an account on behalf of a minor or someone of unsound mind However, it’s not eligible for
o Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) o Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) o Anyone looking to open a joint account, as PPF allows only one account per
individual.
Difference Between NPS and PPF
Here’s a quick NPS and PPF comparison to understand how they serve different types of long-term savers.
Aspect NPS PPF Who’s Eligible? Available to Indian (resident & NRIs) citizens aged 18–70 who complete KYC requirements.
Open to any Indian resident; parents can also create an account for their minor children. Are NRIs Eligible? Yes Not permitted Interest Rates Market-linked returns. PPF earns an interest rate notified by the Government each quarter; currently around 7-8%
Maturity Period No fixed maturity; you can invest until age 60, with the option to continue till 70.
Fixed 15-year lock-in, extendable in 5-year blocks.
Investment Limits
Minimum contribution to open a Tier I account is ₹500 and Tier II account is ₹250. The minimum annual contribution to Tier I account is ₹1000 while there’s no restriction on the contribution in Tier II account.
Minimum ₹500 yearly; maximum ₹1.5 lakh per year with up to 12 deposits allowed.
Tax Benefits Deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80CCD(1) plus an additional ₹50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B).
Contributions qualify for Section 80C deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh; interest and maturity amount are tax-free under the old regime. Withdrawal Rules
You can exit the NPS scheme before 60 prematurely if you have subscribed to the scheme for at least 5 years. On such exit, 20% of the accumulated corpus would be paid as lump sum while the remaining would be used for annuity payments. Exits are also allowed when you reach 60 years of age, between 60 and 75 years of age, or when the subscriber dies, becomes disabled, goes missing, or becomes physically incapacitated. However, the withdrawal rules for each of these instances are different.
Partial withdrawals are allowed after 5 years; loans are available from the second year onward.
Investment Choice
You can pick between equity, government securities, and fixed- income options.
No control over asset allocation.
Return Type Market-driven returns with higher growth potential.
Guaranteed, government-set interest. Annuity Requirement
At least 40% of the corpus must be invested in an annuity unless the total amount is under ₹2 lakh.
Not required.
Similarities Between NPS and PPF
Although NPS or PPF work differently, they share a few core traits since both were designed to support long-term retirement planning:
1. Both function as long-term retirement-focused saving schemes. 2. You need to open an account before you can start contributing. 3. They come with a mandatory lock-in period.
4. Each offers tax advantages to encourage disciplined investing. 5. Returns aren’t taxed while they accumulate. 6. The final corpus is also tax-free at maturity in the case of PPF. For NPS, 60% of the corpus
which is withdrawn in lump sum after 60 is tax-free. 40% of the corpus is used for annuity payouts and the annuity payouts are taxed at your slab rates.
Choosing Between NPS or PPF Based on Your Goals
To select any one of the above investment plans, it’s wise to check how it aligns with your financial objectives:
1. Retirement Planning Perspective
If your main goal is to build a bigger retirement nest egg, NPS generally has an edge over PPF because of its market-linked growth. Historically, NPS equity schemes have delivered higher long‑term returns than fixed‑rate instruments, but future performance may differ.. 2. Risk Appetite and Investment Horizon
NPS comes with some market risk because your money is partly invested in equities. However, it is tightly supervised by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA), with multiple safeguards. PPF, on the other hand, is backed entirely by the government, making its returns nearly risk-free. When it comes to goals, NPS is a better option for long-term retirement planning. However, if you’re saving for other milestones, such as a child’s education or marriage, PPF is usually the more suitable option because it offers stable returns and predictable growth.
Key Takeaway
1. While both are long-term government-backed options, the difference between NPS and PPF
lies in terms of returns, flexibility, and purpose. 2. Regulated by PFRDA, NPS offers Tier I (mandatory) and Tier II (optional) accounts to both
Indians and NRIs, and gives market-linked growth with higher returns. 3. PPF provides guaranteed returns, a fixed 15-year lock-in, tax-free maturity, and is ideal for
risk-averse individual Indian investors. 4. NPS allows partial withdrawals after 10 years, while PPF permits partial access after 5 years
with limits. 5. Both schemes offer tax benefits, but NPS provides an additional deduction of ₹50,000 under
Section 80CCD(1B). 6. Choosing between NPS or PPF depends on your goal; go with PPF for safety or NPS for higher
retirement-focused growth. Conclusion
Choosing between NPS or PPF really comes down to what you want from your money. If long-term growth and a retirement-focused plan appeal to you, NPS offers more potential for growth. But if you prefer stability and guaranteed returns, PPF keeps things simple and predictable. You may split your money between both options if you want a mix of stability and growth. So, choose a suitable option and start planning for retirement or other financial goals. There are online goal-oriented and
retirement calculators that can help you find out the right savings to create a corpus that you need. Use these calculators and plan your finances effectively.
FAQs
1. Can I invest in both NPS and PPF at the same time?
You’re allowed to put money into both NPS and PPF. However, you should know that the combined tax deduction for these investments is capped at ₹1.5 lakh each year. NPS, however, provides an additional ₹50,000 deduction annually under Section 80CCD(1B). 2. Are NPS returns fully tax-free at maturity?
Not entirely. You may withdraw 60% of the NPS corpus tax-free, while 40% should be used to purchase an annuity, and that pension income is taxable. Only if your total corpus is ₹2 lakh or less can the full amount be withdrawn tax-free. 3. What risks are associated with NPS investments?
NPS carries market risk because your money is invested in assets like equities, corporate bonds, and government securities. So, returns can fluctuate depending on how these markets perform. 4. How does PPF differ from NPS in terms of safety?
PPF offers a fixed return set by the government, while NPS, regulated by PFRDA, provides market-linked returns (9-11% in the past). Since the returns are predictable, PPFs are considered safer than NPS. Disclaimer

While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content.
